coredata.dc:title: High quantum yield carbon quantum dots as selective fluorescent turn-off probes for dual detection of Fe2+/Fe3+ ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Siahcheshm, Parya
Heiden, Patricia
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are fluorescent nanoparticles that have great potential for use as nontoxic fluorescent probes. However, synthesizing CQDs with high photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) in a rapid and facile way from commonly available and low-cost reagents is still challenging. In this work, Tween®80 was used as a surface passivation agent along with citric acid/urea in a one-step microwave procedure to make extremely blue luminous nitrogen doped CQDs. When excited at 350 nm, these CQDs had a fluorescent quantum yield of 75.5 %. The significant high quantum yield and stability of these CQDs are attributed to the high aromatic sp2 domain size within the CQD core and improved carbon and oxygen functional groups at the CQDs surface. We employed the CQDs as a fluorescent probe to detect Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions and could establish a linear relationship between photoluminescence quenching efficiency (F0-F/F0) and ion concentration with a detection limit of 6.5 µM and 2.5 µM for Fe2+ and Fe3+, respectively. Our studies suggest that the highly selective detection of iron ions is a result of partial transfers of excited electrons from the CQDs to the d orbital of the iron ions, instead of contributing to radiative relaxation that induces photoluminescence quenching of the CQDs. Other reported carbon dots for this application have a substantially lower quantum yield and only detect Fe3+ ions. Therefore, our CQDs have considerable benefits over similar probes. Subsequent research may adapt them to other areas due to the high quantum yield and stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
High quantum yield
Surface passivation
Microwave synthesis
Fluorescent turn-off probes
Fe2+/Fe3+ ions detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Fe2+/Fe3+ in real samples
Analysis of UV–vis and photoluminescence results
CQDs as a PL sensor for Fe2+/Fe3+Detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Molecular Engineering and Sciences Institute, University of Washington

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge Dr. Anjana Asthana for performing TEM measurement and deeply grateful to Dr. Erico T. F. Freitas for performing EDS measurements. The electron microscopy research was performed at the Applied Chemical and Morphological Analysis Laboratory at Michigan Technological University which is supported by NSF MRI 1429232. The authors express their gratitude to Dr. Samantha L. Young for performing the XPS measurements. Part of this work was conducted at the Molecular Analysis Facility, a National Nanotechnology Coordinated Infrastructure (NNCI) site at the University of Washington, which is supported in part by funds from the National Science Foundation (awards NNCI-2025489, NNCI-1542101), the Molecular Engineering & Sciences Institute, and the Clean Energy Institute. Special thanks to Dr. Shulin Wan and Dr. Wafa Mazi for giving valuable advice on the UV-Vis and PL experiments for QY calculations and metal ion detection. We are also immensely grateful to Dr. Xiaochu Ding for peer reviewing the manuscript and his instructive comments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesizing process of CQDs using microwave synthesis route. (b) HRTEM image of CQDs assembled on a Cu grid with an ultrathin amorphous carbon film. (c)The fast Fourier transform (FFT) image (inset) of a selected area (white square); the lattice parameter is 0.20 nm (d) average particle size distribution of the particles in (b) with Gaussian fitting curve.
(a) FTIR spectra of the synthesized CQDs suggest oxygen functional groups are present on the surface of CQD. High resolution XPS spectra C1s (b), O1s (c), and N1s (d) CQDs.

(a) Shows the UV–vis results for high QY CQDs with digital images under visible light and 365 nm UV light. Photo of CQDs were taken immediately after the reaction is done without further dilution. (b) PL spectra show different emission intensities at different excitation wavelengths. 350 nm UV light induces a most intense PL emission.

(a) Comparison of the PL intensities of 0.05 μg/mL CQDs solution in the presence of different metal ions, at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The error bars for five trials are shown for each column (b) Photos of CQDs (left), CQDs with Fe2+(middle) and CQDs with Fe3+(right) under UV-light (top) and visible light (bottom). The quenching effect of Fe3+ is higher than Fe2+ ions. (c) and (d) are The PL spectra of CQDs with different concentrations of Fe2+ and Fe3+ respectively. (e) and (f) The linear regression plot of CQDs at different concentrations of Fe2+and Fe3+ to calculate LOD.

(a) UV–vis absorption of CQDs before and after addition of Fe ions (b) FTIR spectra of CQDs before and after addition of Fe ions. (c) EDS spectra before and after adding Fe ions, confirming the presence of Fe ions(d), (e) and (f) DLS size analysis of CQDs with and without Fe ions, confirming the formation of aggregates after addition of Fe ions.
(a) UV–vis spectra for tap water with Fe2+ (left) and Fe3+(right) (b The PL spectra of CQDs in tap water with different concentrations of Fe2+ (left) and Fe3+(right).
(a) UV–vis spectra for Portage Lake water with Fe2+ (left) and Fe3+(right) (b The PL spectra of CQDs in Portage Lake water with different concentrations of Fe2+ (left) and Fe3+(right).
Comparison the LOD values of different carbon dot- based fluorescent turn-off probe for detection of Fe ions.
Comparison of calculate QY of CQDs before and after addition of Fe ions.
Sensing Results of Fe ions in Tap water and Portage Lake water samples.

head.ce:title: High quantum yield carbon quantum dots as selective fluorescent turn-off probes for dual detection of Fe2+/Fe3+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Michigan Technological University, 1400 Townsend Drive, Houghton, MI 49931, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Michigan Technological University, 1400 Townsend Drive, Houghton, MI 49931, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are fluorescent nanoparticles that have great potential for use as nontoxic fluorescent probes. However, synthesizing CQDs with high photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) in a rapid and facile way from commonly available and low-cost reagents is still challenging. In this work, Tween®80 was used as a surface passivation agent along with citric acid/urea in a one-step microwave procedure to make extremely blue luminous nitrogen doped CQDs. When excited at 350 nm, these CQDs had a fluorescent quantum yield of 75.5 %. The significant high quantum yield and stability of these CQDs are attributed to the high aromatic sp2 domain size within the CQD core and improved carbon and oxygen functional groups at the CQDs surface. We employed the CQDs as a fluorescent probe to detect Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions and could establish a linear relationship between photoluminescence quenching efficiency (F0-F/F0) and ion concentration with a detection limit of 6.5 µM and 2.5 µM for Fe2+ and Fe3+, respectively. Our studies suggest that the highly selective detection of iron ions is a result of partial transfers of excited electrons from the CQDs to the d orbital of the iron ions, instead of contributing to radiative relaxation that induces photoluminescence quenching of the CQDs. Other reported carbon dots for this application have a substantially lower quantum yield and only detect Fe3+ ions. Therefore, our CQDs have considerable benefits over similar probes. Subsequent research may adapt them to other areas due to the high quantum yield and stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are the most recent nano carbons which were recognized accidentally by Xu et al. in 2004 during the purification of single-wall carbon nanotubes via arc-discharge method [1]. CQDs are known as zero dimensional materials, wherein all the dimensions are measured within the nanoscale and also called quantum dots, with quasi-spherical carbon shape and diameters of below 10 nm. However, a typical size for them is in the range of 2–5 nm. CQDs have high oxygen contents and thus they are also referred to as “carbogenic nanodots”. Usually, the amount of O in CQDs is in the range of 10 %–50 % (wt%). The “O” can be present in the core and/or in functional groups on the surface. However, because the bare surface of CQDs is exposed, it can be contaminated, which may result in low QY CQDs. QY measures the ability of a particle to emit absorbed electromagnetic energy as photons. Because the surfaces of initially synthesized CQDs are not protected they tend to have a low QY.

One way to increase their QY is to protect these surfaces with a surface passivation layer which is a protective layer for the CQD and this increased stability results in a higher QY and long-term use. Surface passivation is achieved when polymeric chains such as polyethylene glycol are attached to the surface of the CQDs to create a thin barrier layer that protects CQDs from impurity adherence and boosts photoluminescence (PL) intensity [2]. The surface passivation creates a thin layer that protects CQDs from impurity adherence and boosts PL intensity. On the other hand, improved QY has also been demonstrated in N-doped CQDs when compared to non-doped CQDs [3–8]. In the N-doped CQDs, some of the carbon atoms are replaced by nitrogen atoms in the core structure, or N-containing groups sit at the edges. The highest QY value that has been reported in the literature for N-doped CQDs is 93.3 % from citric acid and tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane [9]. Among many different reagents, citric acid and urea are considered the most used low-price precursor for CQD synthesis. Citric acid has four active groups (three COOH and one OH), and urea has two amine groups which give water-solubility properties and provide many reactivity sites. Microwave synthesis of CQDs using citric acid and urea is a simple and commonly reported synthetic approach, but the QY is below 10 % [10,11].

One application of high QY carbon dots is as a selective fluorescent turn-off probe for the detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions. The amount of iron ions in environmental water (such as tap water) is an important component in evaluating water quality. Furthermore, iron is a vital nutrient in the human body that plays a critical role in oxygen transport. An iron deficit will result in anemia. On the other hand, excessive amounts of iron may cause tissue damage by catalyzing hydroxyl radicals and speeding up lipid peroxidation [12]. Excess iron also triggers serious diseases, such as increasing the risk of heart failure, cancer, kidney damage, liver cirrhosis, among others [13–16,17]. The World Health Organization (WHO) and the United States Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) drinking water guidelines established the permitted limit of 0.3 mg/L (5.357 µM) iron in drinking water [18,19]. Fe2+ and Fe3+ are the two oxidation states of iron that are actively changing to one another, which makes it difficult to detect Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions only. Therefore, a simple, exact, and rapid total iron (Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions) probing approach is important for the iron analysis of water quality, medical diagnostics, and further quality control tests.

Metal-semiconductor quantum dots have been used in metal ion sensing applications in recent years [20–22] but they typically include heavy metal elements such as Cd, so these dots may be of concern for biological toxicity and environmental problems [23]. Other analytical techniques for iron detection and quantification include electrochemical methods [24], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [25,26], flow injection [27], spectrophotometric sequential injection [28], cathodic stripping voltammetry [29,30], and atomic absorption spectrometry [31,32]. However, these methods require complex instrumentation, time-consuming sample preparation, and extensive procedures. In contrast, fluorescence spectrometry has demonstrated great sensitivity, relatively simple operation, reproducibility, and quick deployment [33]. We anticipate a high QY CQDs with a stable and environmentally benign nature might benefit the fluorescence-based detection for the irons.

Here we report blue-emitting carbon dots from a simple, rapid microwave synthesis using low-cost citric acid, urea, and Tween®80. These carbon dots are stable fluorescent probes that are efficient for the selective detection of Fe2+/Fe3+ ions. The majority of reported carbon dots can only detect Fe3+ ions [4,5,33–42] and there are few number of carbon dots that can detect Fe2+/Fe3+ ions but they suffer from low QY (below 32 %) [17,43,44]. While our CQDs with a QY of 75.5 % detect Fe2+/Fe3+ with a detection limit of 6.5 µM and 2.5 µM respectively.

Reagent grade Tween®80, NaOH (reagent grade, ≥98 %), AgCl (reagent Plus®, 99 %) and ZnCl2 (reagent grade, ≥98 %), citric acid (ACS reagent, ≥99.5 %), were from Sigma urea (>99 %) and quinine sulfate (lab grade, >99 %) were from Fisher Scientific, and all chemicals were used as received. Deionized water (DI-water) was used throughout the research. Other metal salts used in experiments were KCl (>95 %) (From Fisher Science Education™), NaCl, FeSO4·7H2O, Fe (NO3)3·9H2O, Al (NO3)3, MgCl2, CaCl2 (from Flinn scientific) and CuSO4 (from Scholar Chemistry).

An Anton Paar Monowave 450 and glass microwave vials with silicone septum caps were used for the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs. The Horiba Duetta Fluorescence and Absorbance Spectrophotometer were used for Fluorescent and UV–vis analyses. Surface Science Instruments S-Probe spectrometer with 800 × 800 μm spot size was used to get X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) spectra. This XPS uses a monochromatized Al X-ray source with a low energy electron flood gun for charge neutralization under 5 × 10–9 Torr chamber pressure. The pass energy for survey spectra and for high-resolution spectra were 150 eV and 50 eV, respectively. A detailed scan was run for N 1s to improve quantification and all binding energies were calibrated to the C 1s CC bonds at 285.0 eV. Data analysis was carried out using CasaXPS software where a Shirley background was used and the line shape was gaussian lorentzian with 30 % Gaussian character (GL(30)). FEI Titan Themis 200 scanning transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to acquire images with 200 kV input voltage. Size distributions of CQDs were obtained by ImageJ software by first measuring the areas of particles and then calculating the diameter. The TEM samples were prepared by placing a few drops of a highly diluted CQD dispersion on a copper grid with an ultrathin amorphous carbon film which was then dried in the air under a dim light table lamp. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) spectra were collected to characterize the CQDs before and after addition of Fe ions to investigate the binding of Fe ions to CQDs. For EDS analysis, samples were prepared on Silicon Monooxide Type-A 300 mesh, Cu grid instead of ultrathin C film, Cu grid, to avoid presence of C atom peak from substrate. Samples were centrifuged using Beckman Coulter OptimaTM MAX-XP Ultracentrifuge. Samples were dried using Sharp Freeze-110. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) for size analysis were done using Beckman Coulter N4 Plus.

To examine the applications of these CQDs as metal ion probes, PL spectra were measured for the mixture of CQDs/metal ions. All measurements were completed at excitation wavelength λ(ex) of 350 nm in a normal disposable cuvette. To evaluate the selectivity of the CQDs towards Fe2+/Fe3+ sensing, the following different metal ions were tested: Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, K+, Zn2+, Na+, Fe2+, and Fe3+. The concentration of CQDs and metal ions were kept constant at 0.05 μg/mL and 100 µM, respectively, and five trials were performed for every metal ion. For quantitative measurement to get the limit of detection for Fe2+/Fe3+, a series of FeSO4/CQDs and Fe (NO3)3/CQDs solutions were prepared for PL intensity measurements. The concentration of CQDs was kept constant at 0.05 μg/mL, while the concentration of either Fe2+ or Fe3+ varied from 0 to 100 µM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe2+/Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water and lake water (collected from Portage Lake (Houghton, MI)) were tested for sensing of Fe2+/Fe3+. These real water samples were used without further filtration. Exact similar concentration of CQDs (0.05 μg/mL) was added to the real water samples while the concentration of either Fe2+ or Fe3+ varied from 0 to 100 µM. The Fe ion concentrations in real water samples were evaluated by Fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy.

QY was measured using a relative method and a reference compound based on a known procedure [45,46]. The reference compound was selected as quinine sulfate was (QY = 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4). The QY of our sample was then calculated using equation (1):
(1)
Φ =
Φ
ref
×
Grad
Grad
ref
×
n
2
n
r e f 2
where Φ is the QY,
Φ
ref
is the QY of quinine sulfate,
Grad
is the slope from the plot of integrated PL intensity versus absorbance, n is the refractive index (1.33 for both sample and reference). The superscript “ref” refers to quinine sulfate. A series of solutions of CQDs and quinine sulfate was prepared where the absorbance intensities were below 0.1 at λ(ex) of 350 nm. Then, the fluorescence spectra were measured, and the PL intensity was integrated. Later integrated values were plotted versus absorption intensity (Figure S1 (c) and (d)). Finally, the QY for our CQDs was calculated using equation (1) and found to be 75.5 % compared to the QY of otherwise similar CQDs which possessed a QY of 4.2 %. The difference is attributed to the use of Tween®80 as a single reagent synthesized in strong acid [47] As mentioned in the introduction, our CQDs also showed high QY when compared with similar microwave synthesized CQDs based on citric acid/urea with QY below 10 % [10,11].

High QY CQDs were made by adding citric acid and urea in a 1:1 M ratio to 0.038 M Tween® 80 solution at room temperature. Initially 0.038 M Tween®80 solution was made and stirred until clear solution was achieved. Then pre-determined amounts of citric acid and urea were measured and added to 10 mL of the Tween®80 solution, to give 0.1 M solution each. The mixture was then stirred and homogenized by magnetic stirrer operating at a medium stir rate. Then sufficient NaOH was added to give a 0.25 M solution. The mixture was stirred until a clear solution was obtained. The NaOH was added to catalyze carbohydrate carbonization [48]. After mixing the reagents, the solution was transferred to a glass microwave vial with silicone septum caps and reacted at 190C for 30 min in Anton Paar Monowave 450 (Fig. 1 (a)). After the solution was centrifuged at a rate of 25000 rpm for 30 min, the supernatant was collected and was passed through a 0.22 µm sterile PTFE syringe filter (Fisher Scientific). For measuring the mass of synthesized CQDs for detection limit measurements, the solution was further freeze dried (24 h) to remove all the water and the dry CDs were collected. The advantage of this synthesis procedure is using microwave radiation is a rapid, energy efficient method that gives uniform heating [49]. Also, Tween®80 serves as both a C source and surface passing agent to obtain high QY CQDs.

High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) was used to visualize the morphology and size of the synthesized CQDs. Fig. 1 (b) is a typical HRTEM image of the CQDs, and shows that the CQDs exhibit quasi-spherical shapes with a uniform size distribution, and possess a crystal structure as shown by the presence of lattice fringes. In the inset of Fig. 1 (c), the picture of the fast Fourier transform (FFT) (white-dashed square) is shown. The 0.20 nm in-plane lattice spacing matches the (100) crystallographic planes of graphitic carbon [42,50], revealing that these CQDs have a graphite-like structure [51,52]. The average size of the CQDs is 3.42 ± 0.71 nm and its uniform distribution was fitted by a Gaussian curve (Fig. 1 (d)).

The chemical structure of the CQDs was investigated using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2 (a) shows the FTIR spectrum of the synthesized CQDs. The stretching vibrations of the OH are evidenced by a broad band at 3546 cm−1 in the spectra. The aliphatic CH stretching vibrations are at 2926 cm−1. The stretching vibrations of CO in carbonyl groups and aromatic CC stretching vibrations are seen as peaks at 1711 cm−1 and 1518 cm−1, respectively. The CH stretching vibration and aromatic CO stretching vibration are seen in two peaks at 1350 cm−1 and 1250 cm−1, respectively. The stretching vibrations of CO cause the band at 1086 cm−1 [4,34]. The peaks at 949 cm−1 and at 830 cm−1 are corresponding to CH in-plane and benzene out-of-plane bending vibrations [43]. These findings support the presence of oxygen functional groups on the surface of CQDs which account for these CQDs being hydrophilic and water-soluble. The presence of aromatic group vibrations at 2926 cm−1, 1518 cm-1and 830 cm−1 bands supports the graphite-like structure that was observed in TEM and FFT analysis (Fig. 1). The amount of N in the CQDs structure is low (∼1% wt., see Figure S 2 (b)). As a results, it is unlikely that any N functional group would be detected in the FTIR spectra. Thus, although one can attribute the multiple bands between 1208 cm−1 and 1628 cm−1 to the stretching vibration of CN bonds [4] and vibrations at 3546 cm−1, 1518 cm−1, 1086 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1, but in this sample, FTIR really only confirms the presence of OH, CH, CO, CC and CCH groups.

The surface composition/chemical state of the CQDs was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. In the full survey spectra presented in Figure S 2 (b), strong C 1s peak and O 1s peak at 285.2 eV and at 531.2 eV, and a small N 1s peak at 399.2 eV was shown. The O/C atomic ratio for the CQDs was about 33.2 %, while the N/C atomic ratio was calculated to be about 1.1 %. Doping of N groups in the structure of CQDs was also confirmed from FTIR results. The low content of N atoms (from urea) in the resultant CQDs is due to high C and O in the Tween®80 (C64H124O26) compared to the number of N atoms (from urea). Tween®80 is C64H124O26 and citric acid is C₆H₈O₇, compared to urea which is CH4N2O, giving a total of 71 mol of C, 34 mol of O, and only 2 mol of N per 1 mol each of TWEEN®80, citric acid, and urea, or a total mole ratio of 35.5:17:1 of C:O:N assuming 1 mol of each reagent. Tween®80 and citric acid contribute to the formation of the main structure of the CQDs with high contents of C and O.

The high resolution XPS spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. 2 (b)) showed three different peaks for C 1s, corresponding to CC/CC at a binding energy of 284.749 eV, CO/CN at 286.2 eV, and CO/CN at 288.2 eV. The O 1s region in Fig. 2 (c) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 531.1 eV, 533.0 eV, and 533.6 eV, representing CO, CO, and O-CO, respectively. In Fig. 2 (d), the N 1s spectrum comprised four peaks at 398.1, 399.6, 400.1, 402.6, and 403.8 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N, Pyridine N-oxide, and –NO2, respectively [53–57]. These findings support that the hydrothermal microwave reaction leading to the oxygen content in CQDs being present in carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups, as well as some nitrogen functional groups, making them soluble and stable in water. The XPS data also confirm that these CQDs contained an aromatic structure with sp2 domains, and this is also consistent with the FTIR and TEM data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of UV–vis and photoluminescence results
ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous solutions of these CQDs have a brown color under visible light, while under 365 nm UV light, they emit bright intense blue color (inset of Fig. 3 (a)). To investigate the optical properties of the synthesized CQDs and assess the effect of surface passivation (from Tween®80), UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were obtained (Fig. 3). The UV–vis absorption plot had two absorption λ(abs) peaks at 270 and 340 nm, which are attributed to the π–π* transition of CC, and n-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 CO and CN excitation, respectively. Here, CN excitation plays a minor role in the absorption as the N content is low in the CQDs’ structure. The TEM and FTIR data discussed earlier (Fig. 1 and Fig. 2) confirm the presence of aromatic structures in these CQDs. When the UV–vis absorption data are compared to those of CQDs synthesized in the absence of Tween®80 (shown in Figure S 2(a)), a red shift in the λ(abs) of the π–π* transition from 230 nm to 270 nm and n-π* transition from 330 nm to 340 nm was observed. The increase in the π–π* transition is due to the larger aromatic sp2 domain size within the CQD core, which decreases the ground-state band gap [58]. On the other hand, the increase in the n-π* transition is attributed to surface functional groups, and this supports the surface passivation role of Tween®80.

These CQDs exhibit excellent luminescence with the maximum emission wavelength λ(em) at 440 nm (Fig. 3 (b)) when excited at 350 nm. Unlike most other reported CQDs, the PL spectra show excitation independent PL emission properties. This is attributed to narrow size distribution and homogenous surface structure because dependency of the λ(em)max on the λ(ex) is known to result from CQDs having different sizes and/or surface functionality. These results are all in good agreement with results from size and chemical analysis (Fig. 1 and Fig. 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs as a PL sensor for Fe2+/Fe3+Detection
ce:section.ce:para: The detection and subsequent removal of various heavy metal ions, including iron ions, have always been of interest [4,5,17,33–44]. Because iron is a vital mineral that plays an important role in the life and health of humans, its content in biological and environmental systems must be monitored. One method that has been applied by several researchers is to use the quenching property of CQDs, caused by the addition of Fe2+/Fe3+, which is related to the binding affinity of the CQDs with metal ions.

In this study, we investigated the quenching effect of different metal ions with these CQDs by measuring the PL intensity under the same conditions. Fig. 4 (a) shows the effects of different metal ions on the CQDs, including Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, K+, Zn2+, Na+, Fe2+ and Fe3+. The concentration of each metal ion was 100 µM for detection. As shown in Fig. 3b, the excitation wavelength at 350 nm induced the strongest PL emission of the CQDs, so this wavelength was chosen to examine the impact of metal ions on the PL intensity. Upon mixing the CQDs with different metal ions, the PL intensity was significantly quenched only by Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions, whereas other metal ions were largely unaffected (Fig. 4a). Therefore, these CQDs are highly selective in the detection of iron ions.

PL quenching is a result of partial transfer of excited electrons in CQDs to the ions, instead of contributing to radiative relaxation because iron ions show strong affinity towards the hydroxyl/carboxyl groups of CQDs [35,46]. Iron ions can coordinate with CQDs through electron transferring and form a bridge to connect the neighboring hydroxyl/carboxyl groups which result in small local CQDs aggregates. This cause non-radiative electron/hole recombination, resulting in CQDs working as fluorescent turn-off probe for dual detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions.

Fig. 4 (b) shows the photos of pure CQDs with no metal ions (left), CQDs with Fe2+(middle), and CQDs with Fe3+(right) under UV-light (top) and visible light (bottom). The florescent photos demonstrate a stronger quenching effect of Fe3+ than that of Fe2+ ions. This can be related to the higher number of empty orbitals in Fe3+ to interact with the surface hydroxyl/carboxyl groups. The PL spectra of CQDs with different concentrations of both iron ions (Fig. 4 (c) and (d)) show the PL intensity decreases with increasing the concentration. The quenching efficiency was determined by (F0 - F)/F0 , where F0 is the PL intensity of blank CQDs solution and F is the PL intensity of CQDs with different concentrations of metal ions. After plotting the quenching efficiency versus iron concentration, a linear plot is obtained for both iron ions as shown in Fig. 4 (e) and (f). The limit of detection (LOD) can be calculated from LOD = 3.3 σ / m. In this equation, σ is the standard deviation of intercept and m is the slope of the linear regression plot. LOD is calculated as 6.5 µM and 2.5 µM for Fe2+ and Fe3+, respectively. A comparison of this probe with the published work related to fluorescence detection of Fe ions, based on carbon dots is listed in Table 1 . It can be seen that a wide range of LOD has been reported from 13.7 μM to 5 nM and the CQDs in this work were showed an acceptable detection limit which exceeds the limit of iron in drinking water set by the WHO and US EPA (5.537 µM). This result indicated that the CQDs could be potentially used as an optical sensor for the quantitative analysis of Fe ions in an aqueous solution.

To evaluate the effect of Fe on CQDs and their interaction, UV–vis spectra before and after addition of Fe ions were collected. As seen in Fig. 5 (a), the addition of 100 μM Fe ions significantly changed the UV–vis absorption of CQDs, and the absorption at 270 nm and 340 nm vanished. Fe ions may have an impact on the surface states of quantum dots by interacting with surface groups (OH/COOH), which is one probable explanation [67]. The findings above demonstrate that the CQD sensor has a high selectivity for sensing Fe ions.

To further confirm the interaction between Fe ions and surface groups, FTIR spectra of CQDs before and after addition of Fe ions were collected (Fig. 5 (b)). After addition of Fe ions, the stretching vibrations of the OH (evidenced by a broad band at 3546 cm−1) becomes smaller along with the peaks at 1711 cm−1 and 1250 cm−1 corresponding to CO and CO groups, respectively. Moreover, EDS spectra were also collected to confirm the binding between Fe ions and CQDs (Fig. 5 (c)). In order to remove the presence of C peak from the substrate, The Silicon Monoxide/Cu grid was used to prepare the sample. The CQDs sample showed peaks for elements C, O, Si and Cu atoms, where Si and Cu are due to the grid. In addition, a comparable EDS analysis was performed for CQDs with Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions, revealing the additional presence of Fe in the CQDs.

To investigate the formation of aggregates after addition of Fe ions, DLS analysis was done. An average particle size was determined by DLS analysis and is presented in Fig. 5 (d),(e) and (f), showing that the size of CDs, drastically increases after adding Fe ions. Implying that, the addition of Fe ions could induce the fluorescent probes to aggregate, which could be responsible for the PL quenching of CQDs due to the interacting with surface groups (OH/COOH). QY after addition of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions were calculated after related UV–vis and PL measurements and results are presented in the Table 2 , showing that QY decreases due to the quenching effect.

We have demonstrated a rapid synthesis of nitrogen doped CQDs from the carbonization of citric acid/urea/Tween®80 using a simple one-pot hydrothermal microwave procedure. The main purpose of this project was to create a simple and rapid microwave-based hydrothermal method for the synthesis of CQDs with simple formulation using low-cost reagents. The novelty in this work was the use of a surfactant, Tween®80, added with the CQDs’ precursor molecules (citric acid and urea) to stabilize the particles as they were created, and to partially carbonize themselves, onto the surface of the CQDs where they served as passivating agents. The CQDs had a graphitic lattice parameter of 0.20 nm, indicating a graphite-like crystal structure. They were found to be spherical with uniform size and relatively narrow size distribution (3.42 ± 0.71 nm) and consistent surface compositions, resulting in excitation independent PL properties. These CQDs also display exceptionally bright blue-luminescence with excitation independent PL emission features which are due to their uniform size and surface structure. The produced CQDs have a QY of 75.5 % under 350 nm excitation, which is among the greatest QY values for CQDs reported thus far. Furthermore, we have shown that CQDs can be utilized as fluorescent probes to detect both Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions, which is a rarely reported property. This selective and sensitive detection of iron ions is based on the interaction between the Fe ions and the hydroxyl/carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs. This interaction causes a partial transfer of excited electrons from the CQDs to the d orbital of the iron ions, instead of contributing to radiative relaxation. Overall, highly luminescent CQDs synthesized using this method produced CQDs with good water solubility (due to the –OH and –COOH surface functional groups), fluorescence, and optical stability, making them a good candidate for potential applications in Fe ion sensing in medical diagnostics and water quality control tests.

Parya Siahcheshm: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Patricia Heiden: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledgeDr. Anjana Asthana for performing TEM measurement and deeply grateful to Dr. Erico T. F. Freitas for performing EDS measurements. The electron microscopy research was performed at the Applied Chemical and Morphological Analysis Laboratory at Michigan Technological University which is supported by NSF MRI 1429232. The authors express their gratitude to Dr. Samantha L. Young for performing the XPS measurements. Part of this work was conducted at the Molecular Analysis Facility, a National Nanotechnology Coordinated Infrastructure (NNCI) site at the University of Washington, which is supported in part by funds from the National Science Foundation (awards NNCI-2025489, NNCI-1542101), the Molecular Engineering & Sciences Institute, and the Clean Energy Institute. Special thanks to Dr. Shulin Wan and Dr. Wafa Mazi for giving valuable advice on the UV-Vis and PL experiments for QY calculations and metal ion detection. We are also immensely grateful to Dr. Xiaochu Ding for peer reviewing the manuscript and his instructive comments.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.114284.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, C.; Jiang, K.; Xu, Z.; Lin, H.; Zhang, C., Glutathione modified carbon-dots: from aggregation-induced emission enhancement properties to a “turn-on” sensing of temperature/Fe3+ ions in cells. Inorganic Chemistry Frontiers 2016, 3 (4), 514-522.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave-assisted fabrication of multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for iron ions
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sun, Z.; Yang, Z.; Zhao, L.; Zhang, Y.; Li, Y.; Hou, J.; Ding, L., Microwave-assisted fabrication of multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for iron ions. New Journal of Chemistry 2019, 43 (2), 853-861.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A FRET fluorescent nanosensor based on carbon dots for ratiometric detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution
RSC Adv.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Deng, M.; Wang, S.; Liang, C.; Shang, H.; Jiang, S., A FRET fluorescent nanosensor based on carbon dots for ratiometric detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution. RSC Advances 2016, 6 (32), 26936-26940.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-emitting quantum dot/carbon nanodot-based nanoprobe for selective and sensitive detection of Fe(3+) in cells
Analyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, C.; Huang, Y.; Jiang, K.; Humphrey, M. G.; Zhang, C., Dual-emitting quantum dot/carbon nanodot-based nanoprobe for selective and sensitive detection of Fe(3+) in cells. Analyst 2016, 141 (14), 4488-94.

Microchim. Acta

K.; Alle, M.; Lee, S.-H.; Guttena, V., N-Doped carbon dots with pH-sensitive emission, and their application to simultaneous fluorometric determination of iron(III) and copper(II). Microchimica Acta 2020, 187 (1).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic liquid-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iron ion and ascorbic acid
Colloids Surf. A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xie, Z.; Sun, X.; Jiao, J.; Xin, X., Ionic liquid-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iron ion and ascorbic acid. Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects 2017, 529, 38-44.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with chitosan for fluorescent detection of Fe(3)
Polymers (Basel)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao, L.; Wang, Y.; Zhao, X.; Deng, Y.; Xia, Y., Facile Synthesis of Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Quantum Dots with Chitosan for Fluorescent Detection of Fe(3). Polymers (Basel) 2019, 11 (11), 1731.
